Chris Piech  - CS109 class at Stanford

Any measurement, without knowledge of the uncertainty, is meaningless.
But this course is not (just) about error bars…
It’s about understanding how to increase our
In this course we try to master the science of experiment design and obtain estimates we can rely on
But first…. why do we need anything new on top of what we already excel at: eval of large, complex, evolving code bases?
Three Roles for AI in Software
AI is embedded inside your solution
AI helps you write, review, and refactor code. It accelerates development but the output is traditional software.
AI consumes and produces information through your system. It calls your APIs, reads your docs, and acts on your data.
Three Roles for AI in Software
Not in scope – but makes eval even more important:
we iterate more rapidly – if we have good eval signals
– but makes eval even more important:
We can test and collect feedback from our “end users”
When Do You Need AI as a Component in your System?
Behavior is specified, reviewed, and approved
: Billing logic, form validation,  access control
The behavior can be fully written down as rules
But a prompt is simpler than writing code
Outputs are known — you can still test correctness
Behavior is too complex or contextual for rules
Hard to write conventional tests — no expected output
The value comes precisely from what’s hard to specify
E.g.: summarization, entity extraction, open-ended Q&A

The Example of Machine Learning Classifiers
task: classify into ONE of these 5 classes:
how do I get a refund for my last payment?
the plot is predictable and the jokes fall flat
Why do we need ML or Gen AI/LLM?
In the context of classification, we often use
ML to denote the process of learning a function (model) from data, by example
(as opposed to having us humans write the function)
“I want my money back for this order”
Browse a dataset, explore the data
Observe how distributed the labels are.
See how an ML model behaves: try it out yourself
Show that ML treats inputs as points in a hyperspace
Show that in general we do not see the space. We see a sample
We wish we knew the model behavior on the entire space, for every possible input
We have “true” classes only for a
So, when we get an accuracy measure, what do we really know about our system?
Are we willing to stand behind this number and vouch for it?
* the kind of data you will find in production
You have never used this sample to eval your model M before
The sample data is labeled correctly
These assumptions are rarely true – if they are not true, your uncertainty grows, your confidence on the reliability of results goes down
* We will define what “resembles” mean formally later in the course
Under these assumptions, what we can say in this example is:
The accuracy we'd see in production is above 70%
This is where the actual accuracy is likely to be (this is 95% of the “probability mass” resides)

paras.t: Eval: wide range of people and roles
Varying degree of maturity in measurement
Read any AI number as an estimate
Build a test set you can trust
Tell a real improvement from noise
Ask the question that exposes a weak eval
Hands-on. Feel it, don’t just hear it.
Every lesson has an interactive playground — no install, no code.
The aim isn’t to learn statistics — it’s calibrated judgment about AI evidence.
You can't improve what you can't measure.
8 lessons · one source of uncertainty each · understand · report · reduce
Don’t take the number at face value. Understand it, report it, reduce it.
Pick a metric — and read it per class
Five categories, two classifiers, one 5×5 confusion matrix. The diagonal is what's right; zoom into any class for its precision and recall.
One number hides a per-class story
Overall accuracy looks fine — but per class it is uneven. Always look under the headline.
Each dot is a ticket, coloured right/wrong. Right and wrong interlock — no clean boundary — and that doesn't vanish with more data.
We only see the points we labelled
We imagine a smooth competence surface; really we light only our test points and infer the rest of the room.
Re-draw the test set and the score moves
Same fixed model, 2,000 random test sets of 50 — only the draw changed. 78% is one draw: quote n, and a range.
From one result to a credible range
Report n, the 95% credible interval, and P(accuracy > X). The interval narrows as n grows — that is the value of a bigger test set.
Accuracy can flatter a do-nothing model
Skew the test set so one class dominates. A majority-only baseline gains accuracy while its macro-recall stays near 1/5 — a preview of L4.
